comments.body: That's one way to do it. However, toString() should never be relied upon in business logic
@SergeyZolotarev I think the issue is that, while it is easy to parse stuff, it is much harder to determine what to serialize.

answers.body:
Maybe define a toString method that returns the email property of the EmailData class?

@Override
public String toString() {
    return this.email;
}

I suppose this type of mapping is not supported because (my opinion) is quite hard to give a good level of personalization converting multiple values into a single one (how to concat values, how to separate them, etc).

So, if you really want to use mapstruct to build a toString() just provide an implementation to `String toString(EmailData emailData);` by yourself.

comments.body: Yeah, I know. I just wondered whether I had to. Maybe, there's some @MagicAnnotation I could put
I tried some stuff already, including expression = "java(emailData.getEmail())", but the default method is the only thing I got working myself.

answers.body:
Since the mapping is simple enough, just implement it yourself like Luca Basso Ricci already said:

@Mapper
public interface EmailMapper {
    default String toString(EmailData emailData) {
        return emailData == null ? null : emailData.getEmail();
    }

    @Mapping(source = ".", target = "email")
    EmailData toEmailData(String email); // works
}

title: Making MapStruct extract value from source, return as target value
body:
MapStruct can take a plain value and set it to a target object's field.

However, it seems, it can't do the opposite: extract a value and return it as a target object.

import com.example.pixel_user_api.data.entity.EmailData;
import org.mapstruct.Mapper;
import org.mapstruct.Mapping;

@Mapper
public interface EmailMapper {
    @Mapping(source = "email", target = ".")
    String toString(EmailData emailData); // doesn't work

    @Mapping(source = ".", target = "email")
    EmailData toEmailData(String email); // works
}

import lombok.Getter;
import lombok.Setter;

@Getter
@Setter
public class EmailData {
    private Long id;
    private String email;
}

Here's what MapStruct generated:

    @Override
    public String toString(EmailData emailData) {
        if ( emailData == null ) {
            return null;
        }

        String string = new String();

        return string;
    }

    @Override
    public EmailData toEmailData(String email) {
        if ( email == null ) {
            return null;
        }

        EmailData emailData = new EmailData();

        emailData.setEmail( email );

        return emailData;
    }

While toString(EmailData) is straightforward to implement yourself (return emailData.getEmail()), I'm curious: can I make the first method to work too?

A test that has to pass:

import com.example.pixel_user_api.mapper.EmailMapper;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import org.mapstruct.factory.Mappers;

import static org.assertj.core.api.Assertions.assertThat;

class EmailDataTest {
    
    @Test
    void testMapper() {
        String expectedEmail = "example@mail.com";
        EmailData emailData = new EmailData();
        emailData.setEmail(expectedEmail);
        EmailMapper mapper = Mappers.getMapper(EmailMapper.class);
        String actualEmail = mapper.toString(emailData);
        assertThat(actualEmail).isEqualTo(expectedEmail); // fails here
    }
}

org.opentest4j.AssertionFailedError: 
expected: "example@mail.com"
 but was: ""
Expected :"example@mail.com"
Actual   :""

Java 17, MapStruct 1.6.3
